message:
fix(cleanup): harden cleanup_target_path_is_safe against symlink escape

\`cleanup_target_path_is_safe\` decides whether a given filesystem
entry is safe for the doctor/fix reclaimer to delete. It walked the
string paths with \`starts_with\` checks against \`data_dir\`,
\`db_path\`, and \`index_path\`. That was adequate when every path
actually lived under \`data_dir\` as a plain directory entry, but it
gave no protection against two real attack shapes:

1. A symbolic link anywhere between \`data_dir\` and the target
   whose symlink_target points outside of \`data_dir\`. The
   string-level \`starts_with\` check saw the prefix and returned
   \`true\`, so the reclaimer would follow the symlink and
   \`rm -rf\` a directory owned by a different part of the
   filesystem. A malicious or buggy crash of the index writer that
   left a symlink in place could therefore turn a routine
   \`cass doctor --fix\` into destruction of arbitrary state.

2. Paths that string-equal to \`data_dir\` / \`db_path\` /
   \`index_path\` in one normalization but differ in another
   (trailing slashes, unresolved \`..\` segments, different case on
   case-insensitive filesystems, etc.). The reclaimer would then
   treat them as \"not the root\" or \"not the db file\" and
   happily delete them.

Add two defenses inside \`cleanup_target_path_is_safe\`, BEFORE the
existing prefix checks:

- A new helper \`cleanup_path_has_symlink_below_root(path, root)\`
  walks from \`path\` up toward \`root\` one parent at a time.
  Each step calls \`std::fs::symlink_metadata\` — which does not
  dereference symlinks — and returns \`true\` (\"unsafe, bail\")
  the moment it sees any segment that is itself a symlink. The
  walk terminates when it hits \`root\` (safe for this check) or
  runs out of parents (fail closed). The existing caller now
  rejects the target the moment this returns \`true\`.

- After the prefix check, canonicalize \`path\`, \`data_dir\`,
  \`db_path\`, and \`index_path\` via \`std::fs::canonicalize\`. Any
  canonicalize failure on path or data_dir fails closed. The
  canonical path must then \`starts_with\` the canonical data_dir
  and not equal it. Canonical equality with \`db_path\` (when
  canonicalizable) fails closed. Canonical equality or
  prefix-with the index_path (when canonicalizable) fails closed.
  Missing \`db_path\` / \`index_path\` (e.g. fresh install where
  the files do not exist yet) do NOT fail the check on their own,
  which preserves existing behavior on greenfield installs.

Both defenses run BEFORE the pinned-segment / manifest retention
heuristics, so a malicious path can't reach the retention logic
and get whitelisted via a crafted manifest.

No public API change and no change to the happy path for paths
that are actual filesystem entries rooted under \`data_dir\` —
those canonicalize to themselves and pass the prefix check.
